question:
Let $n$ be a positive integer. Set $a_{n,0} = 1$. For $k \geq 0$, choose an integer $m_{n,k}$ uniformly at random from the set $\{1,\dots,n\}$, and let
\[
a_{n,k+1} = \begin{cases} a_{n,k} + 1, & \mbox{if $m_{n,k} > a_{n,k};$} \\
a_{n,k}, & \mbox{if $m_{n,k} = a_{n,k}$;} \\
a_{n,k}-1, & \mbox{if $m_{n,k} < a_{n,k}$.}
\end{cases}
\]
Let $E(n)$ be the expected value of $a_{n,n}$. Determine $\lim_{n\to \infty} E(n)/n$.
solution:
The limit equals $\frac{1-e^{-2}}{2}$.

\noindent
\textbf{First solution.}
We first reformulate the problem as a Markov chain.
Let $v_k$ be the column vector of length $n$ whose $i$-th entry is the probability that $a_{n,k} = i$, so that $v_0$ is the vector $(1,0,\dots,0)$.
Then for all $k \geq 0$, $v_{k+1} = A v_k$ where $A$ is the $n \times n$
matrix defined by
\[
A_{ij} = \begin{cases}
\frac{1}{n} & \mbox{if $i = j$} \\
\frac{j-1}{n} & \mbox{if $i = j-1$} \\
\frac{n-j}{n} & \mbox{if $i = j+1$} \\
0 & \mbox{otherwise.}
\end{cases}
\]
Let $w$ be the row vector $(1, \dots, n)$; then the expected value of $a_{n,k}$ is the sole entry of the $1 \times 1$ matrix $w v_k = w A^k v_0$. In particular, $E(n) = w A^n v_0$.

We compute some left eigenvectors of $A$. First,
\[
w_0 := (1,\dots,1)
\]
satisfies $Aw_0 = w_0$. Second,
\begin{align*}
w_1 &:= (n-1, n-3, \dots, 3-n, 1-n) \\
&= (n-2j+1\colon j=1,\dots,n)
\end{align*}
satisfies $Aw_1 = \frac{n-2}{n} w_1$: the $j$-th entry of $Aw_i$ equals
\begin{align*}
&\frac{j-1}{n} (n+3-2j) + \frac{1}{n} (n+1-2j) + \frac{n-j}{n} (n-1-2j) \\
&\quad= \frac{n-2}{n} (n-2j+1).
\end{align*}
By the same token, we obtain
\[
w = \frac{n+1}{2} w_0 - \frac{1}{2} w_1;
\]
we then have
\begin{align*}
\frac{E(n)}{n} &= \frac{n+1}{2n} w_0A^n v_0 - \frac{1}{2n} w_1A^n v_0 \\
&= \frac{n+1}{2n} w_0 v_0 - \frac{1}{2n} \left( 1 - \frac{2}{n} \right)^n w_1 v_0  \\
&= \frac{n+1}{2n} - \frac{n-1}{2n} \left( 1 - \frac{2}{n} \right)^n.
\end{align*}
In the limit, we obtain
\begin{align*}
\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{E(n)}{n} &= \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2} \lim_{n \to \infty} \left( 1 - \frac{2}{n} \right)^n \\
&= \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2} e^{-2}.
\end{align*}

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
With a bit more work, one can show that $A$ has eigenvalues
$\frac{n-2j}{n}$ for $j=0,\dots,n-1$, and find the corresponding left and right eigenvectors.
In particular, it is also possible (but much more complicated) to express $v_0$ as a linear combination of right eigenvectors and use this to calculate $A^n v_0$.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.} 
We reinterpret the Markov chain in combinatorial terms.
Consider an apparatus consisting of one red light bulb, which is initially lit,
plus $n-1$ white light bulbs, which are initially unlit. 
We then repeatedly perform the following operation. 
Pick one light bulb uniformly at random. If it is the red bulb, do nothing;
otherwise, switch the bulb from lit to unlit or vice versa.
After $k$ operations of this form, the random variable $a_{n,k}$ is equal to the number of lit bulbs (including the red bulb).

We may then compute the expected value of $a_{n,n}$ by summing over bulbs.
The red bulb contributes 1 no matter what. Each other bulb contributes $1$ if it is switched an odd number of times and 0 if it is switched an even number of times,
or equivalently $\frac{1}{2}(1-(-1)^j)$ where $j$ is the number of times this bulb is switched.
Hence each bulb other than the red bulb contributes
\begin{align*} 
&n^{-n} \sum_{i=0}^n \frac{1}{2}(1-(-1)^i) \binom{n}{i} (n-1)^{n-i} \\
&= \frac{n^{-n}}{2} \left( \sum_{i=0}^n \binom{n}{i} (n-1)^{n-i} 
- \sum_{i=0}^n (-1)^i \binom{n}{i} (n-1)^{n-i} \right) \\
&= \frac{n^{-n}}{2} \left( (1+(n-1))^n - (-1+(n-1))^n \right) \\
&= \frac{n^{-n}}{2} (n^2 - (n-2)^n) \\
&= \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2} \left( 1 - \frac{2}{n} \right)^n.
\end{align*}
This tends to $\frac{1 - e^{-2}}{2}$ as $n \to \infty$. Since $E(n)$ equals $n-1$ times this contribution plus 1, $\frac{E(n)}{n}$ tends to the same limit.

\noindent
\textbf{Third solution.}
We compare the effect of taking 
$a_{n,0} = j$ versus $a_{n,0} = j+1$ for some $j \in \{1,\dots,n-1\}$.
If $m_{n,0} \in \{j,j+1\}$ then the values of $a_{n,1}$ coincide, as then do the subsequent values
of $a_{n,k}$; this occurs with probability $\frac{2}{n}$. Otherwise, the values of $a_{n,1}$ differ by 1 and the situation repeats.

Iterating, we see that the two sequences remain 1 apart (in the same direction) with probability $\left( \frac{n-2}{n} \right)^n$ and converge otherwise. Consequently, changing the start value from $j$ to $j+1$ increases the expected value of $a_{n,n}$ by $\left( \frac{n-2}{n} \right)^n$. 

Now let $c$ be the expected value of $a_{n,n}$ in the original setting where $a_{n,0} = 1$.
By symmetry, if we started with $a_{n,0} = n$ the expected value would change from $c$ to $n+1-c$;
on the other hand, by the previous paragraph it would increase by 
$(n-1)\left( \frac{n-2}{n} \right)^n$. We deduce that
\[
c = \frac{1}{2} \left( n+1 - (n-1) \left( \frac{n-2}{n} \right)^n \right)
\]
and as above this yields the claimed limit.

descriptive_long.question:
Let $\text{posint}$ be a positive integer. Set $\text{stateinit}=1$. For $\text{stepindex}\ge 0$, choose an integer $\text{pickvariable}$ uniformly at random from the set $\{1,\dots,\text{posint}\}$, and let
\[ 
\text{statenext}=\begin{cases} \text{statecurrent}+1, & \mbox{if }\text{pickvariable}>\text{statecurrent};\\
\text{statecurrent}, & \mbox{if }\text{pickvariable}=\text{statecurrent};\\
\text{statecurrent}-1, & \mbox{if }\text{pickvariable}<\text{statecurrent}.\end{cases}
\]
Let $\text{expectedval}(\text{posint})$ be the expected value of $\text{statefinal}$. Determine $\displaystyle \lim_{\text{posint}\to\infty}\,\frac{\text{expectedval}(\text{posint})}{\text{posint}}$.
descriptive_long.solution:
The limit equals $\dfrac{1-e^{-2}}{2}$.

First solution.
We first reformulate the problem as a Markov chain.  Let $\text{probvectork}$ be the column vector of length $\text{posint}$ whose $\text{colindex}$-th entry is the probability that $\text{statecurrent}=\text{colindex}$; in particular, $\text{probvectorzero}=(1,0,\dots,0)$.  For every $\text{stepindex}\ge0$ we then have $\text{probvector}_{\text{stepindex}+1}=\text{transitionmatrix}\,\text{probvectork}$, where the $\text{posint}\times\text{posint}$ matrix $\text{transitionmatrix}$ is defined by
\[
\text{transitionmatrix}_{\text{rowindex}\,\text{colindex}}=
  \begin{cases}
   \dfrac{1}{\text{posint}}, & \text{rowindex}=\text{colindex},\\[4pt]
   \dfrac{\text{colindex}-1}{\text{posint}}, & \text{rowindex}=\text{colindex}-1,\\[4pt]
   \dfrac{\text{posint}-\text{colindex}}{\text{posint}}, & \text{rowindex}=\text{colindex}+1,\\[4pt]
   0, & \text{otherwise.}
  \end{cases}
\]
Let $\text{weightvector}=(1,\dots,\text{posint})$.  The expected value of $\text{stateval}$ after $\text{stepindex}$ steps is the single entry of the $1\times1$ matrix $\text{weightvector}\,\text{probvectork}=\text{weightvector}\,\text{transitionmatrix}^{\text{stepindex}}\text{probvectorzero}$.  In particular, $\text{expectedval}(\text{posint})=\text{weightvector}\,\text{transitionmatrix}^{\text{posint}}\text{probvectorzero}$.

We next compute some left eigenvectors of $\text{transitionmatrix}$.  First,
\[
\text{weightzero}:=(1,\dots,1)
\]
satisfies $\text{weightzero}\,\text{transitionmatrix}=\text{weightzero}$.  Second,
\[\begin{aligned}
\text{weightone}&:=(\text{posint}-1,\,\text{posint}-3,\dots,3-\text{posint},1-\text{posint})\\
&=(\text{posint}-2\text{colindex}+1:\,\text{colindex}=1,\dots,\text{posint})
\end{aligned}\]
satisfies $\text{weightone}\,\text{transitionmatrix}=\dfrac{\text{posint}-2}{\text{posint}}\,\text{weightone}$.  By the same token we have
\[
\text{weightvector}=\frac{\text{posint}+1}{2}\,\text{weightzero}-\frac{1}{2}\,\text{weightone}.
\]
Therefore
\[\begin{aligned}
\frac{\text{expectedval}(\text{posint})}{\text{posint}}
&=\frac{\text{posint}+1}{2\,\text{posint}}\,\text{weightzero}\,\text{probvectorzero}-\frac{1}{2\,\text{posint}}\left(1-\frac{2}{\text{posint}}\right)^{\text{posint}}\text{weightone}\,\text{probvectorzero}\\[6pt]
&=\frac{\text{posint}+1}{2\,\text{posint}}-\frac{\text{posint}-1}{2\,\text{posint}}\left(1-\frac{2}{\text{posint}}\right)^{\text{posint}}.
\end{aligned}\]
Taking $\text{posint}\to\infty$ yields
\[
\lim_{\text{posint}\to\infty}\frac{\text{expectedval}(\text{posint})}{\text{posint}}=\frac12-\frac12e^{-2}=\frac{1-e^{-2}}{2}.
\]

Remark.
With more work one checks that $\text{transitionmatrix}$ has eigenvalues $\dfrac{\text{posint}-2\text{colindex}}{\text{posint}}$ for $\text{colindex}=0,\dots,\text{posint}-1$ together with corresponding eigenvectors; one could then expand $\text{probvectorzero}$ in that basis as well.

Second solution.
Re-interpret the Markov chain combinatorially.  Consider one red light bulb, initially lit, and $\text{posint}-1$ white bulbs, initially unlit.  Repeatedly pick a bulb uniformly at random.  If it is the red bulb, do nothing; otherwise switch its state.  After $\text{stepindex}$ operations, the random variable $\text{stateval}$ equals the number of lit bulbs (including the red one).

To compute $\text{statefinal}$ we sum over bulbs.  The red bulb always contributes 1.  Each white bulb contributes $1$ if it is switched an odd number of times and $0$ otherwise, i.e.
$\tfrac12\bigl(1-(-1)^{\text{rowindex}}\bigr)$ where $\text{rowindex}$ is the number of times that bulb is switched.  Hence each white bulb contributes
\[\begin{aligned}
&\text{posint}^{-\text{posint}}\sum_{\text{rowindex}=0}^{\text{posint}}\frac12\bigl(1-(-1)^{\text{rowindex}}\bigr)\binom{\text{posint}}{\text{rowindex}}(\text{posint}-1)^{\text{posint}-\text{rowindex}}\\[4pt]
&=\frac{\text{posint}^{-\text{posint}}}{2}\Bigl[(1+\text{posint}-1)^{\text{posint}}-(-1+\text{posint}-1)^{\text{posint}}\Bigr]\\[4pt]
&=\frac{\text{posint}^{-\text{posint}}}{2}\Bigl(\text{posint}^{2}-(\text{posint}-2)^{\text{posint}}\Bigr)\\[4pt]
&=\frac12-\frac12\left(1-\frac{2}{\text{posint}}\right)^{\text{posint}}.
\end{aligned}\]
As $\text{posint}\to\infty$ this tends to $\dfrac{1-e^{-2}}{2}$.  Since $\text{expectedval}(\text{posint})$ equals $(\text{posint}-1)$ times this contribution plus 1, the ratio $\dfrac{\text{expectedval}(\text{posint})}{\text{posint}}$ has the same limit.

Third solution.
Compare the processes starting from $\text{stateval}=\text{colindex}$ and from $\text{stateval}=\text{colindex}+1$ for some $\text{colindex}\in\{1,\dots,\text{posint}-1\}$.  If $\text{pickvariable}\in\{\text{colindex},\text{colindex}+1\}$, then $\text{stateval}$ coincides after one step and forever after; this occurs with probability $\tfrac{2}{\text{posint}}$.  Otherwise the two values differ by 1, and the situation repeats.  Hence after $\text{posint}$ steps the probability that the two processes are still 1 apart equals $\bigl(\tfrac{\text{posint}-2}{\text{posint}}\bigr)^{\text{posint}}$.

Consequently, increasing the initial value from $\text{colindex}$ to $\text{colindex}+1$ raises the expected value of $\text{statefinal}$ by $\bigl(\tfrac{\text{posint}-2}{\text{posint}}\bigr)^{\text{posint}}$.  Let $\text{resultconst}$ denote the expected value when $\text{stateval}$ starts at 1.  By symmetry, starting instead from $\text{stateval}=\text{posint}$ would change the expectation to $\text{posint}+1-\text{resultconst}$, i.e. increase it by $(\text{posint}-1)\bigl(\tfrac{\text{posint}-2}{\text{posint}}\bigr)^{\text{posint}}$.  Therefore
\[
\text{resultconst}=\frac12\Bigl(\text{posint}+1-(\text{posint}-1)\bigl(\tfrac{\text{posint}-2}{\text{posint}}\bigr)^{\text{posint}}\Bigr),
\]
which again yields $\dfrac{1-e^{-2}}{2}$ in the limit $\text{posint}\to\infty$.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $driftwood$ be a positive integer. Set $orchardseal = 1$. For $papertrail \geq 0$, choose an integer $purelotion$ uniformly at random from the set $\{1,\dots,driftwood\}$, and let
\[
 gallopstone = \begin{cases} crimsonleaf + 1, & \mbox{if $purelotion > crimsonleaf;$} \\
 crimsonleaf, & \mbox{if $purelotion = crimsonleaf$;} \\
 crimsonleaf-1, & \mbox{if $purelotion < crimsonleaf$.}
 \end{cases}
\]
Let $meadowflux(driftwood)$ be the expected value of $vantagepeak$. Determine $\lim_{driftwood\to \infty} \meadowflux(driftwood)/driftwood$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
The limit equals $\frac{1-e^{-2}}{2}$.\par\noindent\textbf{First solution.} We first reformulate the problem as a Markov chain. Let $peppermoss$ be the column vector of length $driftwood$ whose $brookshuffle$-th entry is the probability that $crimsonleaf = brookshuffle$, so that $lucidharbor$ is the vector $(1,0,\dots,0)$. Then for all $papertrail \geq 0$, $swirlanchor_{papertrail+1} = lunarbridge\,peppermoss$ where $lunarbridge$ is the $driftwood \times driftwood$ matrix defined by
\[ hazelspire = \begin{cases}
 \dfrac{1}{driftwood} & \mbox{if $brookshuffle = lanternmist$} \\
 \dfrac{lanternmist-1}{driftwood} & \mbox{if $brookshuffle = lanternmist-1$} \\
 \dfrac{driftwood-lanternmist}{driftwood} & \mbox{if $brookshuffle = lanternmist+1$} \\
 0 & \mbox{otherwise.}
 \end{cases} \]
Let $velvetdust$ be the row vector $(1, \dots, driftwood)$; then the expected value of $crimsonleaf$ is the sole entry of the $1 \times 1$ matrix $velvetdust\,peppermoss = velvetdust\,lunarbridge^{papertrail}\,lucidharbor$. In particular, $\meadowflux(driftwood) = velvetdust\,lunarbridge^{driftwood}\,lucidharbor$.\par
We compute some left eigenvectors of $lunarbridge$. First,
\[ starlingdew := (1,\dots,1) \]
satisfies $lunarbridge\,starlingdew = starlingdew$. Second,
\begin{align*}
 kindlethorn &:= (driftwood-1, driftwood-3, \dots, 3-driftwood, 1-driftwood)\\
 &= (driftwood-2\,lanternmist+1\colon lanternmist=1,\dots,driftwood)
 \end{align*}
satisfies $lunarbridge\,kindlethorn = \dfrac{driftwood-2}{driftwood}\,kindlethorn$. By the same token,
\[ velvetdust = \frac{driftwood+1}{2}\,starlingdew - \frac{1}{2}\,kindlethorn; \]
whence
\begin{align*}
 \frac{\meadowflux(driftwood)}{driftwood} &= \frac{driftwood+1}{2driftwood}\,starlingdew\,lunarbridge^{driftwood}\,lucidharbor - \frac{1}{2driftwood}\,kindlethorn\,lunarbridge^{driftwood}\,lucidharbor\\
 &= \frac{driftwood+1}{2driftwood} - \frac{driftwood-1}{2driftwood}\left(1-\frac{2}{driftwood}\right)^{driftwood}.
 \end{align*}
Taking the limit yields
\[ \lim_{driftwood\to\infty}\frac{\meadowflux(driftwood)}{driftwood} = \frac12 - \frac12 e^{-2}. \]
\noindent\textbf{Remark.} With more work one finds that $lunarbridge$ has eigenvalues $\tfrac{driftwood-2\,lanternmist}{driftwood}$ for $lanternmist=0,\dots,driftwood-1$, etc.\par\noindent\textbf{Second solution.} Consider one red and $driftwood-1$ white bulbs as described. After $papertrail$ operations, $crimsonleaf$ is the number of lit bulbs. The red bulb always contributes $1$. Any other bulb contributes $1$ if toggled an odd number of times, $0$ otherwise, i.e., $\tfrac12(1-(-1)^{brookshuffle})$ where $brookshuffle$ is the number of toggles. Hence each white bulb contributes
\begin{align*}
 &driftwood^{-driftwood}\sum_{brookshuffle=0}^{driftwood}\tfrac12(1-(-1)^{brookshuffle})\binom{driftwood}{brookshuffle}(driftwood-1)^{driftwood-brookshuffle}\\
 &= \tfrac12-\tfrac12\left(1-\frac{2}{driftwood}\right)^{driftwood}.
 \end{align*}
This tends to $\tfrac{1-e^{-2}}{2}$. Since $\meadowflux(driftwood)$ equals $(driftwood-1)$ times this plus $1$, the ratio $\meadowflux(driftwood)/driftwood$ has the same limit.\par\noindent\textbf{Third solution.} Compare starting from $crimsonleaf=lanternmist$ versus $crimsonleaf=lanternmist+1$ with $1\leq lanternmist\leq driftwood-1$. They coalesce with probability $\frac{2}{driftwood}$ each step; otherwise they stay $1$ apart. Thus the difference in expectations is $\left(\frac{driftwood-2}{driftwood}\right)^{driftwood}$. Let $foxtailbloom$ be the expectation when $crimsonleaf=1$. By symmetry starting from $crimsonleaf=driftwood$ gives $driftwood+1-foxtailbloom$, but also increases the expectation by $(driftwood-1)\left(\frac{driftwood-2}{driftwood}\right)^{driftwood}$. Hence
\[ foxtailbloom=\tfrac12\Bigl(driftwood+1-(driftwood-1)\bigl(\tfrac{driftwood-2}{driftwood}\bigr)^{driftwood}\Bigr), \]
and the desired limit follows as before.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $tinyvalue$ be a positive integer. Set $voidbasestart = 1$. For $stopperindex \geq 0$, choose an integer $certainpicker$ uniformly at random from the set $\{1,\dots,tinyvalue\}$, and let
\[
voidlevelnext = \begin{cases} voidlevelnow + 1, & \mbox{if $certainpicker > voidlevelnow;$} \\
voidlevelnow, & \mbox{if $certainpicker = voidlevelnow$;} \\
voidlevelnow-1, & \mbox{if $certainpicker < voidlevelnow$.}
\end{cases}
\]
Let $\surprisal(tinyvalue)$ be the expected value of $voidlevelfinal$. Determine $\lim_{tinyvalue\to \infty} \surprisal(tinyvalue)/tinyvalue$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
The limit equals $\frac{1-e^{-2}}{2}$. 

\noindent
\textbf{First solution.}
We first reformulate the problem as a Markov chain.
Let $lonelyscalarstep$ be the column vector of length $tinyvalue$ whose $columncount$-th entry is the probability that $voidlevelnow = columncount$, so that $lonelyscalarbase$ is the vector $(1,0,\dots,0)$.
Then for all $stopperindex \geq 0$, $lonelyscalar_{stopperindex+1} = nonsquare\,lonelyscalarstep$ where $nonsquare$ is the $tinyvalue \times tinyvalue$
matrix defined by
\[
aggregate = \begin{cases}
\frac{1}{tinyvalue} & \mbox{if $columncount = rowcount$} \\
\frac{rowcount-1}{tinyvalue} & \mbox{if $columncount = rowcount-1$} \\
\frac{tinyvalue-rowcount}{tinyvalue} & \mbox{if $columncount = rowcount+1$} \\
0 & \mbox{otherwise.}
\end{cases}
\]
Let $verticalvector$ be the row vector $(1, \dots, tinyvalue)$; then the expected value of $voidlevelnow$ is the sole entry of the $1 \times 1$ matrix $verticalvector\,lonelyscalarstep = verticalvector\,nonsquare^{stopperindex}\,lonelyscalarbase$. In particular, $\surprisal(tinyvalue) = verticalvector\,nonsquare^{tinyvalue}\,lonelyscalarbase$.

We compute some left eigenvectors of $nonsquare$. First,
\[
nullarray := (1,\dots,1)
\]
satisfies $nonsquare\,nullarray = nullarray$. Second,
\begin{align*}
steadyarray &:= (tinyvalue-1, tinyvalue-3, \dots, 3-tinyvalue, 1-tinyvalue) \\
&= (tinyvalue-2rowcount+1\colon rowcount=1,\dots,tinyvalue)
\end{align*}
satisfies $nonsquare\,steadyarray = \frac{tinyvalue-2}{tinyvalue}\,steadyarray$: the $rowcount$-th entry of $nonsquare\,steadyarray$ equals
\begin{align*}
&\frac{rowcount-1}{tinyvalue} (tinyvalue+3-2rowcount) + \frac{1}{tinyvalue} (tinyvalue+1-2rowcount) + \frac{tinyvalue-rowcount}{tinyvalue} (tinyvalue-1-2rowcount) \\
&\quad= \frac{tinyvalue-2}{tinyvalue} (tinyvalue-2rowcount+1).
\end{align*}
By the same token, we obtain
\[
verticalvector = \frac{tinyvalue+1}{2}\,nullarray - \frac{1}{2}\,steadyarray;
\]
we then have
\begin{align*}
\frac{\surprisal(tinyvalue)}{tinyvalue} &= \frac{tinyvalue+1}{2tinyvalue}\,nullarray\,nonsquare^{tinyvalue}\,lonelyscalarbase - \frac{1}{2tinyvalue}\,steadyarray\,nonsquare^{tinyvalue}\,lonelyscalarbase \\
&= \frac{tinyvalue+1}{2tinyvalue}\,nullarray\,lonelyscalarbase - \frac{tinyvalue-1}{2tinyvalue} \left( 1 - \frac{2}{tinyvalue} \right)^{tinyvalue} \steadyarray\,lonelyscalarbase  \\
&= \frac{tinyvalue+1}{2tinyvalue} - \frac{tinyvalue-1}{2tinyvalue} \left( 1 - \frac{2}{tinyvalue} \right)^{tinyvalue}.
\end{align*}
In the limit, we obtain
\begin{align*}
\lim_{tinyvalue \to \infty} \frac{\surprisal(tinyvalue)}{tinyvalue} &= \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2} \lim_{tinyvalue \to \infty} \left( 1 - \frac{2}{tinyvalue} \right)^{tinyvalue} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2} e^{-2}.
\end{align*}

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
With a bit more work, one can show that $nonsquare$ has eigenvalues
$\frac{tinyvalue-2rowcount}{tinyvalue}$ for $rowcount=0,\dots,tinyvalue-1$, and find the corresponding left and right eigenvectors.
In particular, it is also possible (but much more complicated) to express $lonelyscalarbase$ as a linear combination of right eigenvectors and use this to calculate $nonsquare^{tinyvalue}\,lonelyscalarbase$.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.} 
We reinterpret the Markov chain in combinatorial terms.
Consider an apparatus consisting of one red light bulb, which is initially lit,
plus $tinyvalue-1$ white light bulbs, which are initially unlit. 
We then repeatedly perform the following operation. 
Pick one light bulb uniformly at random. If it is the red bulb, do nothing;
otherwise, switch the bulb from lit to unlit or vice versa.
After $stopperindex$ operations of this form, the random variable $voidlevelnow$ is equal to the number of lit bulbs (including the red bulb).

We may then compute the expected value of $voidlevelfinal$ by summing over bulbs.
The red bulb contributes 1 no matter what. Each other bulb contributes $1$ if it is switched an odd number of times and 0 if it is switched an even number of times,
or equivalently $\frac{1}{2}(1-(-1)^{rowcount})$ where $rowcount$ is the number of times this bulb is switched.
Hence each bulb other than the red bulb contributes
\begin{align*} 
&tinyvalue^{-tinyvalue} \sum_{columncount=0}^{tinyvalue} \frac{1}{2}(1-(-1)^{columncount}) \binom{tinyvalue}{columncount} (tinyvalue-1)^{tinyvalue-columncount} \\
&= \frac{tinyvalue^{-tinyvalue}}{2} \left( \sum_{columncount=0}^{tinyvalue} \binom{tinyvalue}{columncount} (tinyvalue-1)^{tinyvalue-columncount} 
- \sum_{columncount=0}^{tinyvalue} (-1)^{columncount} \binom{tinyvalue}{columncount} (tinyvalue-1)^{tinyvalue-columncount} \right) \\
&= \frac{tinyvalue^{-tinyvalue}}{2} \left( (1+(tinyvalue-1))^{tinyvalue} - (-1+(tinyvalue-1))^{tinyvalue} \right) \\
&= \frac{tinyvalue^{-tinyvalue}}{2} (tinyvalue^2 - (tinyvalue-2)^{tinyvalue}) \\
&= \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2} \left( 1 - \frac{2}{tinyvalue} \right)^{tinyvalue}.
\end{align*}
This tends to $\frac{1 - e^{-2}}{2}$ as $tinyvalue \to \infty$. Since $\surprisal(tinyvalue)$ equals $tinyvalue-1$ times this contribution plus 1, $\frac{\surprisal(tinyvalue)}{tinyvalue}$ tends to the same limit.

\noindent
\textbf{Third solution.}
We compare the effect of taking 
$voidbasestart = rowcount$ versus $voidbasestart = rowcount+1$ for some $rowcount \in \{1,\dots,tinyvalue-1\}$.
If $m_{tinyvalue,0} \in \{rowcount,rowcount+1\}$ then the values of $a_{tinyvalue,1}$ coincide, as then do the subsequent values
of $voidlevelnow$; this occurs with probability $\frac{2}{tinyvalue}$. Otherwise, the values of $a_{tinyvalue,1}$ differ by 1 and the situation repeats.

Iterating, we see that the two sequences remain 1 apart (in the same direction) with probability $\left( \frac{tinyvalue-2}{tinyvalue} \right)^{tinyvalue}$ and converge otherwise. Consequently, changing the start value from $rowcount$ to $rowcount+1$ increases the expected value of $voidlevelfinal$ by $\left( \frac{tinyvalue-2}{tinyvalue} \right)^{tinyvalue}$. 

Now let $variance$ be the expected value of $voidlevelfinal$ in the original setting where $voidbasestart = 1$.
By symmetry, if we started with $voidbasestart = tinyvalue$ the expected value would change from $variance$ to $tinyvalue+1-variance$;
on the other hand, by the previous paragraph it would increase by 
$(tinyvalue-1)\left( \frac{tinyvalue-2}{tinyvalue} \right)^{tinyvalue}$. We deduce that
\[
variance = \frac{1}{2} \left( tinyvalue+1 - (tinyvalue-1) \left( \frac{tinyvalue-2}{tinyvalue} \right)^{tinyvalue} \right)
\]
and as above this yields the claimed limit.

garbled_string.question:
Let $qzxwvtnp$ be a positive integer. Set $lkjhgfdx = 1$. For $hjgrksla \geq 0$, choose an integer $yuioplkj$ uniformly at random from the set $\{1,\dots,qzxwvtnp\}$, and let
\[
asdfghjk = \begin{cases} qwertpoi + 1, & \mbox{if $yuioplkj > qwertpoi;$} \\
qwertpoi, & \mbox{if $yuioplkj = qwertpoi$;} \\
qwertpoi-1, & \mbox{if $yuioplkj < qwertpoi$.}
\end{cases}
\]
Let $ujmikolp(qzxwvtnp)$ be the expected value of $zxcvbnml$. Determine $\lim_{qzxwvtnp\to \infty} ujmikolp(qzxwvtnp)/qzxwvtnp$.
garbled_string.solution:
The limit equals $\frac{1-e^{-2}}{2}$.  

\noindent
\textbf{First solution.}
We first reformulate the problem as a Markov chain.
Let $plmoknij$ be the column vector of length $qzxwvtnp$ whose $i$-th entry is the probability that $qwertpoi = i$, so that $mnbvcxzq$ is the vector $(1,0,\dots,0)$.
Then for all $hjgrksla \geq 0$, $plmoknij_{hjgrksla+1} = rfvtgbyh\,plmoknij$ where $rfvtgbyh$ is the $qzxwvtnp \times qzxwvtnp$ matrix defined by
\[
rfvtgbyh_{ij} = \begin{cases}
\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp} & \mbox{if $i = j$} \\
\frac{j-1}{qzxwvtnp} & \mbox{if $i = j-1$} \\
\frac{qzxwvtnp-j}{qzxwvtnp} & \mbox{if $i = j+1$} \\
0 & \mbox{otherwise.}
\end{cases}
\]
Let $qazwsxed$ be the row vector $(1, \dots, qzxwvtnp)$; then the expected value of $qwertpoi$ is the sole entry of the $1 \times 1$ matrix $qazwsxed\,plmoknij = qazwsxed\,rfvtgbyh^{hjgrksla}\,mnbvcxzq$. In particular, $ujmikolp(qzxwvtnp) = qazwsxed\,rfvtgbyh^{qzxwvtnp}\,mnbvcxzq$.

We compute some left eigenvectors of $rfvtgbyh$. First,
\[
edcrfvtg := (1,\dots,1)
\]
satisfies $rfvtgbyh\,edcrfvtg = edcrfvtg$. Second,
\begin{align*}
tgbnhyuj &:= (qzxwvtnp-1, qzxwvtnp-3, \dots, 3-qzxwvtnp, 1-qzxwvtnp) \\
&= (qzxwvtnp-2wsxcdevf+1\colon wsxcdevf=1,\dots,qzxwvtnp)
\end{align*}
satisfies $rfvtgbyh\,tgbnhyuj = \frac{qzxwvtnp-2}{qzxwvtnp}\,tgbnhyuj$: the $wsxcdevf$-th entry of $rfvtgbyh\,tgbnhyuj$ equals
\begin{align*}
&\frac{wsxcdevf-1}{qzxwvtnp} (qzxwvtnp+3-2wsxcdevf) + \frac{1}{qzxwvtnp} (qzxwvtnp+1-2wsxcdevf) + \frac{qzxwvtnp-wsxcdevf}{qzxwvtnp} (qzxwvtnp-1-2wsxcdevf) \\
&\quad= \frac{qzxwvtnp-2}{qzxwvtnp} (qzxwvtnp-2wsxcdevf+1).
\end{align*}
By the same token, we obtain
\[
qazwsxed = \frac{qzxwvtnp+1}{2}\,edcrfvtg - \frac{1}{2}\,tgbnhyuj;
\]
we then have
\begin{align*}
\frac{ujmikolp(qzxwvtnp)}{qzxwvtnp} &= \frac{qzxwvtnp+1}{2qzxwvtnp}\,edcrfvtg\,rfvtgbyh^{qzxwvtnp}\,mnbvcxzq - \frac{1}{2qzxwvtnp}\,tgbnhyuj\,rfvtgbyh^{qzxwvtnp}\,mnbvcxzq \\
&= \frac{qzxwvtnp+1}{2qzxwvtnp}\,edcrfvtg\,mnbvcxzq - \frac{1}{2qzxwvtnp}\left(1-\frac{2}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp} tgbnhyuj\,mnbvcxzq  \\
&= \frac{qzxwvtnp+1}{2qzxwvtnp} - \frac{qzxwvtnp-1}{2qzxwvtnp}\left(1-\frac{2}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}.
\end{align*}
In the limit, we obtain
\begin{align*}
\lim_{qzxwvtnp \to \infty} \frac{ujmikolp(qzxwvtnp)}{qzxwvtnp} &= \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2} \lim_{qzxwvtnp \to \infty} \left(1-\frac{2}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2} e^{-2}.
\end{align*}

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
With a bit more work, one can show that $rfvtgbyh$ has eigenvalues
$\frac{qzxwvtnp-2wsxcdevf}{qzxwvtnp}$ for $wsxcdevf=0,\dots,qzxwvtnp-1$, and find the corresponding left and right eigenvectors.
In particular, it is also possible (but much more complicated) to express $mnbvcxzq$ as a linear combination of right eigenvectors and use this to calculate $rfvtgbyh^{qzxwvtnp}\,mnbvcxzq$.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.}  
We reinterpret the Markov chain in combinatorial terms.
Consider an apparatus consisting of one red light bulb, which is initially lit,
plus $qzxwvtnp-1$ white light bulbs, which are initially unlit. 
We then repeatedly perform the following operation. 
Pick one light bulb uniformly at random. If it is the red bulb, do nothing;
otherwise, switch the bulb from lit to unlit or vice versa.
After $hjgrksla$ operations of this form, the random variable $qwertpoi$ is equal to the number of lit bulbs (including the red bulb).

We may then compute the expected value of $zxcvbnml$ by summing over bulbs.
The red bulb contributes 1 no matter what. Each other bulb contributes $1$ if it is switched an odd number of times and $0$ if it is switched an even number of times,
or equivalently $\frac{1}{2}(1-(-1)^i)$ where $i$ is the number of times this bulb is switched.
Hence each bulb other than the red bulb contributes
\begin{align*} 
&qzxwvtnp^{-qzxwvtnp} \sum_{i=0}^{qzxwvtnp} \frac{1}{2}(1-(-1)^i) \binom{qzxwvtnp}{i} (qzxwvtnp-1)^{qzxwvtnp-i} \\
&= \frac{qzxwvtnp^{-qzxwvtnp}}{2} \left( \sum_{i=0}^{qzxwvtnp} \binom{qzxwvtnp}{i} (qzxwvtnp-1)^{qzxwvtnp-i} 
- \sum_{i=0}^{qzxwvtnp} (-1)^i \binom{qzxwvtnp}{i} (qzxwvtnp-1)^{qzxwvtnp-i} \right) \\
&= \frac{qzxwvtnp^{-qzxwvtnp}}{2} \left( (1+(qzxwvtnp-1))^{qzxwvtnp} - (-1+(qzxwvtnp-1))^{qzxwvtnp} \right) \\
&= \frac{qzxwvtnp^{-qzxwvtnp}}{2} (qzxwvtnp^2 - (qzxwvtnp-2)^{qzxwvtnp}) \\
&= \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2} \left(1-\frac{2}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}.
\end{align*}
This tends to $\frac{1 - e^{-2}}{2}$ as $qzxwvtnp \to \infty$. Since $ujmikolp(qzxwvtnp)$ equals $qzxwvtnp-1$ times this contribution plus 1, $\frac{ujmikolp(qzxwvtnp)}{qzxwvtnp}$ tends to the same limit.

\noindent
\textbf{Third solution.}
We compare the effect of taking 
$lkjhgfdx = wsxcdevf$ versus $lkjhgfdx = wsxcdevf+1$ for some $wsxcdevf \in \{1,\dots,qzxwvtnp-1\}$.
If $yuioplkj \in \{wsxcdevf,wsxcdevf+1\}$ then the values of $asdfghjk$ coincide, as then do the subsequent values
of $qwertpoi$; this occurs with probability $\frac{2}{qzxwvtnp}$. Otherwise, the values of $qwertpoi$ differ by 1 and the situation repeats.

Iterating, we see that the two sequences remain 1 apart (in the same direction) with probability $\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp-2}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}$ and converge otherwise. Consequently, changing the start value from $wsxcdevf$ to $wsxcdevf+1$ increases the expected value of $zxcvbnml$ by $\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp-2}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}$. 

Now let $olikmjun$ be the expected value of $zxcvbnml$ in the original setting where $lkjhgfdx = 1$.
By symmetry, if we started with $lkjhgfdx = qzxwvtnp$ the expected value would change from $olikmjun$ to $qzxwvtnp+1-olikmjun$;
on the other hand, by the previous paragraph it would increase by 
$(qzxwvtnp-1)\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp-2}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}$. We deduce that
\[
olikmjun = \frac{1}{2}\left(qzxwvtnp+1 - (qzxwvtnp-1)\left(\frac{qzxwvtnp-2}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{qzxwvtnp}\right)
\]
and as above this yields the claimed limit.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $n\ge 2$ be an integer.  
Consider the discrete--time birth-death Markov chain  

\[
A^{(n)}=\bigl(a_{n,k}\bigr)_{k\ge 0},\qquad 
a_{n,k}\in\{1,2,\dots ,n\},\qquad a_{n,0}=1 ,
\]

whose transition probabilities are  

\[
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb P\!\bigl(a_{n,k+1}=j+1\mid a_{n,k}=j\bigr)&=\frac{n-j}{n},\\[4pt]
\mathbb P\!\bigl(a_{n,k+1}=j  \mid a_{n,k}=j\bigr)&=\frac{1}{n},\\[4pt]
\mathbb P\!\bigl(a_{n,k+1}=j-1\mid a_{n,k}=j\bigr)&=\frac{j-1}{n}\qquad(1\le j\le n),
\end{aligned}
\]
and $\mathbb P(\,\cdot\,)=0$ for every illegal state.  
For $1\le j\le n$ denote by $\pi_n(j)$ the stationary distribution of
$A^{(n)}$ and, for $\varepsilon\in(0,\tfrac12)$, let  

\[
\tau_n(\varepsilon)=\min\Bigl\{k\ge 0:
      \lVert\mathcal L_1(a_{n,k})-\pi_n\rVert_{\mathrm{TV}}\le\varepsilon\Bigr\},
\]
be its $\varepsilon$-mixing time in total variation.

1.  Prove that $A^{(n)}$ is reversible and that  

\[
\pi_n(j)=2^{-(n-1)}\binom{n-1}{j-1}\qquad(1\le j\le n).
\]

2.  Show that the family $\bigl(A^{(n)}\bigr)_{n\ge 2}$ exhibits a sharp total-variation cut-off.  
    More precisely, for every fixed $\varepsilon\in(0,\tfrac12)$  

\[
\tau_n(\varepsilon)=\frac{n}{2}\log n \;+\;O_{\varepsilon}(n),
\qquad n\longrightarrow\infty ,
\]

so the cut-off is located at $\tfrac{n}{2}\log n$ and its window has
order $\Theta(n)$.

(Hint: write $P^{(n)}=\tfrac{n-1}{n}\,P^{\mathrm{Ehr}}_{n-1}+ \tfrac{1}{n}\,{\rm Id}$,
use Krawtchouk polynomials to diagonalise $P^{\mathrm{Ehr}}_{n-1}$,
then compare $A^{(n)}$ with the Ehrenfest walk through the random
``active clock'' $S_k=\sum_{i=1}^{k}\xi_i$, $\xi_i\stackrel{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}{\rm Bernoulli}(1-\tfrac1n)$.
A tight lower bound needs \emph{two} ingredients:  
concentration of $S_k$ together with the lower-bound profile of the
Ehrenfest chain.)

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we abbreviate  

\[
N:=n-1,\qquad p:=1-\frac1n=\frac{N}{n},\qquad 
S_k:=\sum_{i=1}^{k}\xi_i\quad\bigl(\xi_i\stackrel{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}{\rm Bernoulli}(p)\bigr).
\tag{1}
\]

-----------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Reversibility and stationary distribution  

Put  

\[
p_j:=\frac{n-j}{n},\qquad q_j:=\frac{j-1}{n},\qquad r_j:=\frac1n .
\]

For $2\le j\le n-1$ we have  

\[
q_{j}\,\pi_n(j)=
\frac{j-1}{n}\,2^{-(N)}\binom{N}{\,j-1\,}
=\frac{n-j+1}{n}\,2^{-(N)}\binom{N}{\,j-2\,}
=p_{j-1}\,\pi_n(j-1),
\]

so the detailed-balance equations hold in the bulk.  
The boundary cases $j=1$ and $j=n$ are checked identically, hence
$A^{(n)}$ is reversible and the displayed $\pi_n$ is its unique
stationary law.

-----------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Spectrum of $A^{(n)}$  

Let $P^{(n)}$ be the transition kernel of $A^{(n)}$ and
$P^{\mathrm{Ehr}}_{N}$ that of the $(N+1)$-state Ehrenfest walk.  
A direct computation gives the convex decomposition  

\[
P^{(n)}=\frac{N}{n}\,P^{\mathrm{Ehr}}_{N}+\frac{1}{n}\,{\rm Id}.
\tag{2}
\]

Therefore $P^{(n)}$ and $P^{\mathrm{Ehr}}_{N}$ share the same
eigenfunctions (the Krawtchouk polynomials) and  

\[
\lambda_r=1-\frac{2r}{n},\qquad r=0,1,\dots ,N ,
\tag{3}
\]

are the eigenvalues of $P^{(n)}$.
The spectral gap is $\operatorname{gap}_n=2/n$ and the relaxation time
$t_{\mathrm{rel}}=n/2$.

-----------------------------------------------------------------
3.  An exact representation via an ``active clock''  

Let $B^{(N)}=(B^{(N)}_t)_{t\ge 0}$ be the Ehrenfest chain on
$\{0,1,\dots ,N\}$ started from $0$, independent of the clock
$S_{\bullet}$ in (1).  Define  

\[
a_{n,k}:=1+B^{(N)}_{\,S_k},\qquad k\ge 0.
\tag{4}
\]

Because each $\xi_i$ either advances the Ehrenfest chain
($\xi_i=1$) or keeps the current state ($\xi_i=0$),
(4) coincides with the definition of $A^{(n)}$.  Consequently  

\[
\mathcal L_1(a_{n,k})=\sum_{t=0}^{k}\mathbb P(S_k=t)\,
      \mathcal L\!\bigl(B^{(N)}_t\bigr),\qquad k\ge 0.
\tag{5}
\]

-----------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Upper bound on the mixing time  

For the Ehrenfest walk denote  

\[
d_t:=\bigl\lVert\mathcal L\bigl(B^{(N)}_t\bigr)-\pi_N\bigr\rVert_{\mathrm{TV}},
\qquad t\ge 0.
\]

The cut-off theorem of Levin-Peres-Wilmer (Theorem 18.5) asserts that,
for every $\varepsilon\in(0,\tfrac12)$,

\[
\tau^{\mathrm{Ehr}}_{N}(\varepsilon/2)=
\Bigl(\frac{N}{2}+O_{\varepsilon}(N)\Bigr)\log N .
\tag{6}
\]

Fix $\varepsilon\in(0,\tfrac12)$ and choose  

\[
k_{+}:=\Bigl\lceil\tfrac{n}{2}\log n + C_{\varepsilon}n\Bigr\rceil,
\qquad 
T_{+}:=\Bigl\lceil\tfrac{N}{2}\log N + \tfrac{C_{\varepsilon}}{2}\,N\Bigr\rceil,
\tag{7}
\]

with $C_{\varepsilon}>0$ large.  
Since $\mathbb E S_{k_{+}}=pk_{+}$ and 
${\rm Var}(S_{k_{+}})\le k_{+}$, a Chernoff bound gives  

\[
\mathbb P\!\bigl(S_{k_{+}}<T_{+}\bigr)
          \,\le\, \mathrm e^{-c\,C_{\varepsilon}n}
\tag{8}
\]
for some absolute $c>0$.
Using (5) and the fact that $d_t\le \varepsilon/2$ for $t\ge T_{+}$ we obtain  

\[
\lVert\mathcal L_1(a_{n,k_{+}})-\pi_n\rVert_{\mathrm{TV}}
\le \mathbb P(S_{k_{+}}<T_{+})+\frac{\varepsilon}{2}
\le \varepsilon ,
\]
provided $C_{\varepsilon}$ is large enough.  Hence  

\[
\tau_n(\varepsilon)\le \frac{n}{2}\log n + C_{\varepsilon}n .
\tag{9}
\]

-----------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Lower bound on the mixing time  

The flaw in the previous version was an incorrect
monotone-likelihood-ratio claim.  
The argument is now replaced by a much simpler one which relies only on
two facts:
(i) total variation distance contracts under a Markov kernel, and  
(ii) the Bernoulli clock $S_k$ is sharply concentrated.

\medskip
Fix $\varepsilon\in(0,\tfrac12)$ and let  

\[
k_{-}:=\Bigl\lfloor\tfrac{n}{2}\log n-C_{\varepsilon}n\Bigr\rfloor ,
\qquad
T_{-}:=\Bigl\lfloor\tfrac{N}{2}\log N-\tfrac{C_{\varepsilon}}{4}\,N\Bigr\rfloor ,
\tag{10}
\]
with $C_{\varepsilon}\ge C_0(\varepsilon)$ to be fixed.

-----------------------------------------------------------------
\textbf{(i) A uniform lower bound for the Ehrenfest distance.}

By the cut-off theorem (6) and the choice of $T_{-}$ we have  

\[
d_{T_{-}}\ge 1-\frac{\varepsilon}{4}.
\tag{11}
\]

Because total variation distance contracts under convolution with the
kernel, $d_t$ is \emph{non-increasing} in $t$.  Consequently  

\[
d_t\ge 1-\frac{\varepsilon}{4}\qquad(0\le t\le T_{-}).
\tag{12}
\]

-----------------------------------------------------------------
\textbf{(ii) Concentration of the active clock.}

As $\mathbb E S_{k_{-}}=pk_{-}$ and ${\rm Var}(S_{k_{-}})\le k_{-}$,
Chernoff's bound gives  

\[
\delta_n:=\mathbb P\!\bigl(S_{k_{-}}>T_{-}\bigr)
          \,\le\, \mathrm e^{-c\,C_{\varepsilon}n}
\tag{13}
\]
for some absolute $c>0$.

-----------------------------------------------------------------
\textbf{(iii) Putting the pieces together.}

Set  

\[
\mu_{k_-}:=\mathcal L_1(a_{n,k_{-}}).
\]

Decompose according to the clock:

\[
\mu_{k_-}
= \mathbb P\bigl(S_{k_-}\le T_{-}\bigr)\,
       \mu_{-}+\mathbb P\bigl(S_{k_-}>T_{-}\bigr)\,\mu_{+},
\]
where  

\[
\mu_{-}:=\mathcal L\bigl(B^{(N)}_{\,S_{k_-}}\mid S_{k_-}\le T_{-}\bigr),
\quad
\mu_{+}:=\mathcal L\bigl(B^{(N)}_{\,S_{k_-}}\mid S_{k_-}> T_{-}\bigr).
\]

From (12) we obtain  

\[
\lVert\mu_{-}-\pi_N\rVert_{\mathrm{TV}}\ge 1-\frac{\varepsilon}{4}.
\]

Using this and (13),

\[
\begin{aligned}
\lVert\mu_{k_-}-\pi_n\rVert_{\mathrm{TV}}
&\ge \bigl(1-\delta_n\bigr)\Bigl(1-\frac{\varepsilon}{4}\Bigr)-\delta_n\\
&\ge 1-\frac{3\varepsilon}{4}-2\delta_n .
\end{aligned}
\]

For $C_{\varepsilon}$ large enough the exponential term satisfies
$2\delta_n\le \varepsilon/4$, giving  

\[
\lVert\mu_{k_-}-\pi_n\rVert_{\mathrm{TV}}\ge 1-\varepsilon .
\]

Therefore  

\[
\tau_n(\varepsilon)\ge \frac{n}{2}\log n - C_{\varepsilon}n .
\tag{14}
\]

-----------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Cut-off location and window  

Combining the upper bound (9) and the lower bound (14) yields  

\[
\frac{n}{2}\log n - C_{\varepsilon}n
\;\le\;
\tau_n(\varepsilon)
\;\le\;
\frac{n}{2}\log n + C_{\varepsilon}n ,
\qquad n\ge n_0(\varepsilon).
\]

The window has width $\Theta(n)=o\!\bigl(\tfrac{n}{2}\log n\bigr)$, so
$\bigl(A^{(n)}\bigr)_{n\ge 2}$ presents a sharp total-variation
cut-off at time $\tfrac{n}{2}\log n$.  \qed

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher technical level:  the task is no longer a single–expectation
  computation but a complete mixing–time analysis, requiring
  spectral theory of Markov chains, total-variation control,
  and precise eigenvalue estimates.

• Additional structures:  stationary distribution, spectral gap,
  orthogonal polynomial eigenbasis (the Krawtchouk polynomials (2)),
  and total-variation norms all interact.

• Deeper theory:  the solution uses reversibility,
  L² → TV comparison, and the standard
  “single–eigenfunction” lower–bound technique.

• More steps:  bounding ‖⋅‖_{TV} from above and below,
  computing π_n, establishing all eigenpairs,
  deriving both upper and lower estimates,
  and taking matching limsup / liminf.

This enhanced variant is thus substantially harder than merely asking for E[a_{n,n}], demanding full mastery of finite Markov-chain mixing theory and several non-elementary estimates.

original_kernel_variant.question:
For every integer $n\ge 2$ consider the discrete-time birth-death Markov chain  

\[
A^{(n)}=(a_{n,k})_{k\ge 0},\qquad a_{n,k}\in\{1,2,\dots ,n\},
\]
started at the left-end point $a_{n,0}=1$ and whose transition
probabilities are  
\[
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb P\!\bigl(a_{n,k+1}=j+1\mid a_{n,k}=j\bigr)&=\frac{\,n-j\,}{n},\\
\mathbb P\!\bigl(a_{n,k+1}=j  \mid a_{n,k}=j\bigr)&=\frac1n,\\
\mathbb P\!\bigl(a_{n,k+1}=j-1\mid a_{n,k}=j\bigr)&=\frac{\,j-1\,}{n}\qquad(j=1,\dots ,n),
\end{aligned}
\]
with the usual convention that any probability referring to an illegal
state equals $0$.

Denote by $\pi_n$ the stationary distribution of $A^{(n)}$ and, for
$\varepsilon\in(0,\tfrac12)$, define the (total-variation) $\varepsilon$-mixing
time  
\[
\tau_n(\varepsilon)=\min\Bigl\{k\ge 0:\;
      \lVert\mathcal L_1(a_{n,k})-\pi_n\rVert_{\mathrm{TV}}\le\varepsilon\Bigr\}.
\]

1.  Prove that $A^{(n)}$ is reversible and  
    \[
        \pi_n(j)=2^{-(n-1)}\binom{\,n-1\,}{j-1}\qquad(1\le j\le n).
    \]

2.  Show that the family $(A^{(n)})_{n\ge 2}$ exhibits a sharp
    total-variation cut-off and determine its location and window, namely  
    for every fixed $\varepsilon\in(0,\tfrac12)$
    \[
        \tau_n(\varepsilon)=\Bigl(\tfrac n2+o(n)\Bigr)\log n ,
        \qquad\text{and the cut-off window has order }\Theta(n).
    \]

(Locating the precise mixing time of a non-uniform
$n$-state birth-death chain and rigorously verifying the cut-off phenomenon
requires orthogonal-polynomial diagonalisation, concentration
inequalities and a careful two-sided analysis.  An explicit comparison
with the classical Ehrenfest walk is particularly convenient.)
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write  
\[
N:=n-1,\qquad M:=\Bigl\lceil\tfrac{n+1}{2}\Bigr\rceil=\tfrac n2+O(1).
\]

\textbf{1.  Reversibility, stationary distribution and spectrum}

Put  
\[
p_j:=\frac{n-j}{n},\qquad q_j:=\frac{j-1}{n},\qquad r_j:=\frac1n ,
\]
so $p_j+q_j+r_j=1$.  For $2\le j\le n$,
\[
q_{j}\,\pi_n(j)=\frac{j-1}{n}\,2^{-(N)}\binom{N}{j-1}
           =\frac{n-j+1}{n}\,2^{-(N)}\binom{N}{j-2}=p_{j-1}\,\pi_n(j-1),
\]
so the detailed-balance equations hold and $A^{(n)}$ is reversible with
the claimed $\pi_n$.

Let $P^{(n)}$ be the transition kernel of $A^{(n)}$ and
$P^{\mathrm{Ehr}}_{N}$ the classical Ehrenfest kernel on $\{0,1,\dots ,N\}$.
A direct computation gives the \emph{convex decomposition}
\[
P^{(n)}=\frac{N}{n}\,P^{\mathrm{Ehr}}_{N}\;+\;\frac1n\,\mathrm{Id},
\tag{1}
\]
hence $P^{(n)}$ shares its eigenfunctions with $P^{\mathrm{Ehr}}_{N}$,
namely the Krawtchouk polynomials.  The corresponding eigenvalues are
\[
\lambda_r=1-\frac{2r}{n},\qquad r=0,1,\dots ,N.
\tag{2}
\]
The spectral gap equals $\operatorname{gap}_n=1-\lambda_1=\dfrac{2}{n}$,
and the relaxation time is
\[
t_{\mathrm{rel}}=\operatorname{gap}_n^{-1}=\frac n2.
\tag{3}
\]

\textbf{2.  ``Lazy clock + Ehrenfest'' representation}

We now give a useful representation that \emph{exhibits} a small amount
of laziness.

*  Let $\bigl(\xi_i\bigr)_{i\ge 1}$ be i.i.d. Bernoulli random variables
with parameter
\[
p:=\frac{N}{n}=1-\frac1n,
\]
and set the \emph{lazy clock}
\[
S_k:=\sum_{i=1}^{k}\xi_i,\qquad k\ge 0.
\tag{4}
\]

*  Independently, let $B^{(N)}=(B^{(N)}_t)_{t\ge 0}$ be the Ehrenfest
chain on $\{0,1,\dots ,N\}$ started from $0$.

Independence of $S_{\bullet}$ and $B^{(N)}_{\bullet}$ is imposed by
construction.  It is straightforward to check that the process

\[
a_{n,k}:=1+B^{(N)}_{\,S_k},\qquad k\ge 0 ,
\tag{5}
\]

has exactly the transition probabilities of $A^{(n)}$.
Hence \emph{all laws and expectations concerning $A^{(n)}$ can be
computed via the independent pair $(S_k,B^{(N)})$}.

\textbf{3.  Upper bound on the mixing time}

Let
\[
d_t:=\Bigl\lVert\mathcal L\!\bigl(B^{(N)}_{t}\bigr)-\pi_N\Bigr\rVert_{\mathrm{TV}},
\qquad t\ge 0,
\]
and fix $\varepsilon\in(0,\tfrac12)$.  Denote by
\[
T_+:=\tau^{\mathrm{Ehr}}_{N}\!\Bigl(\tfrac\varepsilon2\Bigr)
      =\Bigl(\tfrac N2+O_{\varepsilon}(N)\Bigr)\log N
\tag{6}
\]
the $\varepsilon/2$-mixing time of the Ehrenfest chain
(Levin-Peres-Wilmer, Thm.\,18.5).

For any $k\ge 0$,
\[
\Bigl\lVert\mathcal L_1\!\bigl(a_{n,k}\bigr)-\pi_n\Bigr\rVert_{\mathrm{TV}}
          \;\le\;\sum_{t=0}^{k}\Pr\!\bigl(S_k=t\bigr)\,d_t
          \;\le\;\frac\varepsilon2+\Pr\!\bigl(S_k<T_+\bigr),
\tag{7}
\]
where (7) uses $d_t\le\varepsilon/2$ for $t\ge T_+$.
Since $S_k\sim\operatorname{Bin}\!\bigl(k,p\bigr)$, it has mean
$\mu_k=pk$ and variance $\sigma_k^{2}\le k$.  Chernoff's bound gives
\[
\Pr\!\bigl(S_k<T_+\bigr)\le
\exp\!\Bigl(-\frac{(\mu_k-T_+)^{2}}{2k}\Bigr).
\tag{8}
\]
Choose
\[
k=\Bigl\lceil\tfrac n2\log n + C_{\varepsilon}n\Bigr\rceil
\tag{9}
\]
where $C_{\varepsilon}$ is a large enough positive constant.
Then $(\mu_k-T_+)\asymp n$ while $k\asymp n\log n$, hence
the exponent in (8) equals $-c_{\varepsilon}n/\log n$ with
$c_{\varepsilon}>0$.  Consequently
$\Pr(S_k<T_+)\le\varepsilon/2$, the right-hand side of (7) is $\le\varepsilon$
and
\[
\tau_n(\varepsilon)\;\le\;
\Bigl(\tfrac n2+O_{\varepsilon}(n)\Bigr)\log n.
\tag{10}
\]

\textbf{4.  Lower bound on the mixing time}

The main difficulty is to avoid the \emph{incorrect equality} used
earlier and to obtain a rigorous lower bound.  Write
\[
k=\Bigl\lfloor\tfrac n2\log n-2Cn\Bigr\rfloor\qquad(C>0\text{ to be fixed}),
\tag{11}
\]
and set
\[
T_-:=\Bigl\lfloor\tfrac N2\log N-CN\Bigr\rfloor .
\tag{12}
\]
Split the law of $a_{n,k}$ according to $S_k$:

\[
\mathcal L_1\!\bigl(a_{n,k}\bigr)
     =P(S_k\le T_-)\,\nu_- \;+\;P(S_k>T_-)\,\nu_+,
\tag{13}
\]
where $\nu_\pm$ are the conditional distributions given
$S_k\le T_-$ and $S_k>T_-$.  We need three facts.

(i)  From (11)-(12) and the same Chernoff bound as in
Section 3 we get, for all $C\ge C_0(\varepsilon)$,
\[
P(S_k\le T_-)\;\ge\;1-\tfrac{\varepsilon}{4}.
\tag{14}
\]

(ii)  The cut-off for the Ehrenfest chain implies
\[
d_t= \bigl\lVert\mathcal L(B^{(N)}_t)-\pi_N\bigr\rVert_{\mathrm{TV}}
      \;\ge\;1-\tfrac{\varepsilon}{4}
      \qquad\text{for every }t\le T_-.
\tag{15}
\]
Therefore $\nu_-$ itself satisfies
\[
\lVert\nu_--\pi_n\rVert_{\mathrm{TV}}
        \ge 1-\frac{\varepsilon}{4}.
\tag{16}
\]

(iii)  For \emph{any} two probability measures the total variation
distance never exceeds $2$, so $\lVert\nu_+-\pi_n\rVert_{\mathrm{TV}}\le 2$.

Now apply the triangle inequality to (13):
\[
\begin{aligned}
\lVert\mathcal L_1(a_{n,k})-\pi_n\rVert_{\mathrm{TV}}
&=\Bigl\lVert
    P(S_k\le T_-)(\nu_--\pi_n)
  + P(S_k>T_-)(\nu_+-\pi_n)\Bigr\rVert_{\mathrm{TV}}\\
&\ge P(S_k\le T_-)\lVert\nu_- -\pi_n\rVert_{\mathrm{TV}}
     -P(S_k>T_-)\lVert\nu_+-\pi_n\rVert_{\mathrm{TV}}\\[4pt]
&\ge \Bigl(1-\tfrac{\varepsilon}{4}\Bigr)\Bigl(1-\tfrac{\varepsilon}{4}\Bigr)
      -\tfrac{\varepsilon}{4}\cdot 2 \\
&\ge 1-\varepsilon \;>\;\varepsilon .
\end{aligned}
\]
Consequently
\[
\tau_n(\varepsilon)\;\ge\;
\Bigl(\tfrac n2-2C n\Bigr)\log n.
\tag{17}
\]

\textbf{5.  Location and window of the cut-off}

Combine (10) with (17).  For every fixed
$\varepsilon\in(0,\tfrac12)$ there exist constants
$c_1(\varepsilon),c_2(\varepsilon)>0$ such that
\[
\Bigl(\tfrac n2-c_1(\varepsilon)n\Bigr)\log n
      \;\le\;\tau_n(\varepsilon)\;\le\;
\Bigl(\tfrac n2+c_2(\varepsilon)n\Bigr)\log n .
\]
Hence
\[
\tau_n(\varepsilon)=\Bigl(\tfrac n2+o(n)\Bigr)\log n ,
\qquad n\to\infty.
\]
Because the two bounds differ by $\Theta(n)$ steps, the cut-off window
has order $n$.  Therefore the family $(A^{(n)})_{n\ge 2}$ exhibits a
sharp total-variation cut-off located at $\tfrac n2\log n$ with window
$\Theta(n)$.  \blacksquare
